This is "The Song of Songs," excellent above all others, because it describes entirely the excellences of Christ and the love between him and his redeemed people.

The church, or rather the believer, speaks here in the character of the King’s spouse, the Messiah. The kisses of his mouth mean those assurances of pardon with which believers are favored, filling them with peace and joy in believing, and causing them to abound in hope by the power of the Holy Spirit. People who love God greatly take great pleasure in loving Christ and being loved by him. Christ's love is more valuable and desirable than anything this world can give. The name of Christ is not now like ointment sealed up, but like ointment poured out; which shows how freely and fully his grace is made known through the gospel. Those whom he has redeemed and sanctified are here the virgins who love Jesus Christ and follow him wherever he goes. They ask him to draw them by the powerful influences of his Spirit. The more clearly we understand Christ's glory, the more aware we will be that we cannot adequately follow him, while also wanting to do so. Observe the quick answer given to this prayer. Those who wait at Wisdom’s gate will be led into truth and comfort. And when they are brought into this chamber, their sorrows will vanish. We have joy only in Christ, and for this we thank him. We will remember to give thanks for your love; it will leave a lasting impression on us more than anything else in this world. Only sincere love is acceptable to Christ. The daughters of Jerusalem may mean people who profess faith but are not yet firmly established. The spouse was dark as the tents of wandering Arabs, but beautiful as the magnificent curtains in Solomon’s palaces. The believer is dark because of being defiled and sinful by nature, but beautiful because renewed by God's grace into his holy image. He still bears traces of sin, but he is beautiful because accepted in Christ. He is often seen as unimportant by people, but he is excellent in God’s sight. His darkness came from the harsh treatment he had suffered. The children of the church, her mother, but not of God, her Father, were angry with her. They made her suffer hardships, which caused her to neglect caring for her soul. Thus, under the image of a poor woman who is the chosen partner of a prince, we consider the circumstances in which Christ’s love finds its objects. They were miserable slaves to sin, in toil or sorrow, weary and burdened, but what a change when Christ's love is revealed to their souls!

Observe the title given to Christ: O You whom my soul loves. Those who do so may come to him boldly and humbly plead with him. Are God’s people experiencing a time of outward trouble and inner conflict? Christ offers them rest. Those whose souls love Jesus Christ earnestly desire to share in the privileges of his flock. Turning away from Christ is what those who love God greatly fear more than anything else. God is ready to answer prayer. Follow the familiar path, ask for the old way, observe the footsteps of the flock, and see how people have lived righteously. Sit under the guidance of good ministers, beside the tents of the shepherds. Bring your children with you; they will all be welcome. It will be the sincere desire and prayer of every Christian that God would direct him in his work and order his life so he can always have his Lord and Savior before him.

The Bridegroom gives high praise to his spouse. In Christ’s sight, believers are excellent people, fit to promote his glory. The spiritual gifts and graces which Christ bestows on every true believer are described by the ornaments then in use. The graces of the saints depend on each other. He who began this good work will finish it. The grace received from Christ's fullness overflows into lively expressions of faith, affection, and gratitude. Yet Christ himself is more precious than his gifts. The word translated "camphire" means “atonement or propitiation.” Christ is dear to all believers because he is the atoning sacrifice for their sins. No one else can take his place in the soul. They resolved to lodge him in their hearts throughout life’s troubles. Christ delights in the good work that his grace has done on the souls of believers. This should encourage everyone who has been made holy to be very thankful for the grace that has made them beautiful, when they were naturally deformed. The spouse (the believer) has a humble and modest look, showing simplicity and sincere devotion; eyes enlightened and guided by the Holy Spirit, that blessed Dove. The church expresses her appreciation for Christ: You are the great original, but I am only a faint and imperfect copy. Many people may be beautiful to look at, but their character makes them unpleasant; but Christ is both beautiful and pleasant. The believer speaks with praise of those sacred practices in which true believers have fellowship with Christ. Whether the believer is in the presence of God, or in quiet solitude, whether doing daily work, suffering sickness, or even imprisoned, a sense of God’s presence will turn that place into paradise. Thus, the soul, having daily fellowship with the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, enjoys a hopeful expectation of an incorruptible, undefiled, and unfading inheritance above.
